summary: Gruber delivers an exhaustive critique of the Safari 4 public beta, praising WebKit's performance gains while cataloging serious UI regressions. He argues the new tabs-in-title-bar design, inspired by Google Chrome but taken a step too far, creates click-through problems, breaks window management conventions, and uses non-standard colors that undermine Leopard's consistent visual language. The replacement of the determinate progress bar with an indeterminate spinner makes Safari feel slower despite being faster. He acknowledges good additions like improved auto-completion, Cover Flow for history, and Top Sites, but concludes the tab redesign was over-engineered to solve the wrong problem.
key_points: WebKit's zero-tolerance policy for performance regressions is the foundation of Safari's speed advantage, and the team has maintained it for over six years
The new indeterminate progress spinner removes useful feedback and makes Safari feel slower despite being objectively faster
Safari 4's tabs-in-title-bar goes further than Chrome's implementation, creating click-through problems, breaking drag conventions, and muddling the window-vs-tab conceptual model
Safari 4's non-standard window colors undermine Leopard's system-wide consistency for distinguishing foreground and background windows
The simpler solution for encouraging tab adoption would have been enabling the existing tab bar by default rather than redesigning the entire title bar
money_quotes: The way to make a program faster is to never let it get slower.
The most common excuse is one that the WebKit policy doesn't list: 'We'll fix the performance issues later.' The truth is that sometimes, later never comes.
The new progress spinner doesn't make Safari slower, but it does make it feel slower.
A determinate progress meter has the psychological effect of making a wait seem shorter.
Safari 4's tabs-in-the-title-bar arrangement is like a book with text set right to the very edge of the paper — it saves space at the expense of something useful.
In Safari 4, if you want to move the window you drag a tab, and if you want to move a tab you drag the small grippy strip at the far right edge of a tab. This is more abstract, indirect, and worse.
Hiding the controls doesn't eliminate the actual clutter — a Safari 4 title bar containing five or six tabs is littered with dangerous spots on which to click or drag.
If you never remove lesser-used features, you can't add new features without letting the overall complexity blow up.
key_insight: Safari 4's UI redesign sacrifices established conventions and user feedback for marginal space savings, demonstrating how over-engineering a solution to encourage tab adoption created more problems than it solved.
